sentences.text: Is he here?
Yeah, he just joined.
And then, … He also invited Alec, who's a, asset manager for them.
Don't let him in.
Okay, I'll wait.
No, I'm just kidding.
Oh.
No, I love it.
Hey, Scott!
How you doing?
Oh, you brought the beep guns!
We had to.
What's up, Scott?
How you doing, buddy?
I'm doing better now!
Damn, you keep it classy.
Every time I get on a call with you, I'm like, why is this guy so much classier than I am?
It's not that, it's the… that's the rules, the rules of the office!
I'm so disappointed you didn't tell me about this.
I had to find out about this product myself.
Yeah, I guess we're missing on that.
You know, we've been keeping it pretty quiet.
Oh, yeah?
But, … Yeah, I mean, I think we only notified just a few, few customers.
We've just been building alongside a few people, and… But now it's… it's getting ready to go… go for the big time.
Right?
Yeah.
And Scott, this is the genuine, honest truth.
Hunter, and I were talking 3 days before this got scheduled, and I said, you should reach out to Scott.
And… I'm gonna throw Hunter under the bus, he hadn't reached out to you yet, so….
I don't believe that, Corey, and I'll tell you why, because, … What's her name?
My, apartment night.
Thank you, girl.
Larissa?
We talked about it, like, 3 weeks ago, and she said, oh, I'm gonna mention it to them and get you set up.
So, huh.
Larissa reached out to us.
Three days before that, Hunter and I had talked, and I said, reach out to Scott.
So, I don't know what happened.
We should have for the original one.
I had to blow you guys off one time, that's my fault.
That's why when you were making me wait, I thought it was payback.
I don't know, Corey and I had the conversation, and I gotta defend myself.

You were on the list, I was going through all of the clients I've signed up, and the goal was to pick the best-fitting ones to have the call with, and you were on the list, I was still trying to finalize who I was gonna initially send this to, since we're not kind of, like, blasting this out to everybody.
And then next thing you know, the invite comes through for the call.
So, the good news is.
You know where I, … so I don't… I don't do any social media except LinkedIn.
And I actually saw it on LinkedIn.
I saw a post from you, Alec, I think, on, on LinkedIn.
That's what kind of… and then I asked Larissa about it.
Got it.
It's great!
LinkedIn.
Excited!
So, was this, … is this something you were always working on, or was it something that sort of came out of the RealPage….
Class action stuff.
We… we always had plans.

Just because we felt like… It wasn't, you know… Arum… some people like Arum, but we… I always got the sense people weren't in love with it, and that, you know, they were… RealPage was deprecating LRO, and so it always sort of felt like, to be honest with you, you know, I came up on the advertising side of multifamily.
It always felt like the way people feel about Apartments.
com and CoStar.
They're sort of like, I've gotta do it, but I don't like it.
And so, I always felt like there was an opportunity because most of this stuff, I'm actually one of the guys that built Yieldstar originally lives in town.
I had lunch with him, and he was… I was like, how old are the algorithms?
He's like.
25 years old.
So, … I always felt like we could do it, and then, yeah, over the last 18 months, I mean.
We've just had a ton of customers approach us, and, you know, they're trying to get off RealPage.
They need a public alternative, and so that's really what kind of accelerated it.
So I looked at, I guess about a year ago, Alec, would you say?
I looked at… we looked at, like.
Because we, we, we were getting, … we were getting letters from the property managers that the onsites… their on-site staff would not work with RealPage, with Yieldstar.
Which is obviously a problem for us.
So, I looked at all kinds of different… scenarios, and different products, and vendors, and so on.
And then, … And then, of course, it came up that you could… you could turn off the… Non-public data, which we did.
And it didn't really hurt us, because we had so much history with the properties.
I think if you didn't have a lot of history, I think it may have affected you, but it didn't affect us at all.

But I, yeah, I looked at a lot of different stuff, and the one thing I did learn is that it's, … similar to, like, if you were to move away from Yardi, it's, … Like, the operating pla- like, it's… it's a big thing to get off what you're used to for… 10 years, you know, I think?

And every… of course, everybody… all you salespeople, it's all easy, we're there to support you, blah blah blah, which is all bullshit, because once you get… it's like buying a car, once you get past the sales department, it's all downhill from there.
So, it's a bit scary, to be honest with you.

I'm just being transparent with you, it's a bit scary to, to move away from something Now the other thing is, you know, what I didn't get to, what I would have liked to have done is sort of run… To… kind of simultaneously.
you know, like, run a Yield Star and run something else, and at the same time, and kind of see you know, compare them.
Never, never really got to that point.
Yep.
Did you get close, or what did you like that you saw out there?
… What was that one, Alec, that, … That weirdo from Austin was… Promoting.
It wasn't… it wasn't the Yardy one, right?
No, no, no, it wasn't Yardi.
… They're a smaller company.
The problem is they don't… you have to use their…
They didn't have pricing, like a pricing manager.
I can't remember what it was called.
I don't see your… I don't see them on LinkedIn anymore, maybe somebody bought them.
You know, … what the hell was her name?
She used to work for Bainbridge in Austin.
And then she went out, became, like, a… Consultant.
Oh.
I remember the demo.
The weird hair.
You know, the weird….
hair, and she's got a pet pig, and all that stuff.
Ugh.
Anyhow, that's probably the closest we got.
Not important, I guess.
I was more curious, conceptually, what you liked, and what you were maybe drawn to from… maybe that.
We liked it because it was… it was… It's probably simpler for the PMs than… Daniel Star?
Which is important for us, right?
If we're gonna make them use something, it's gotta be… You know, it's gotta be pretty straightforward for them.
I think….
It was the dashboard, right?
The dashboard….
Yeah, it was just easier to run.
Yardi's was completely, … Complicated, so that wasn't gonna work.
I… I use… I use Yardi in my… previous life in Canada.
What's up?
Oh my god.
It's gonna come to me.
I'm googling this aggressively right now to see if I can… it was at Bainbridge?
I lived in Austin.
She was at Bainbridge, yeah.
What Bainbridge didn't even realize is she was… she was building out her own sort of business and product while she was working for Bainbridge.
Yeesh.
… You know Alec, what the hell's her name?
Is that Washburn?
It starts with an S, I think.
I don't see her on LinkedIn anymore, which is really weird.
I was gonna say, I don't know of a single… I don't know of a revenue management company out of Austin.
Well, she… she wasn't… she wasn't the rev management company, she introduced me to them, but she was… she was trained and authorized by them to be a pricing manager.
So you had to… you had to pay… For the product, and then you had to pay, goddamn, whatever her name is, for… to be your pricing manager.
Got it.
Yeah.
Nice.
And then there's those other guys… I don't really hear them… from them much anymore, but they wanna…
I think our owners invested in them or something, that's how they keep calling me, but they want to put their product on top of your rev management product.
And they say they'll get… earn you even better NOI.
I can't remember the name of them either, but….
Interesting.
What did you find, like, when you started to think about what the transition would be, what did you think was going to be the most painful?
Switching.
The tran… the transition.
Yeah.
Like, you gotta remember, Og, I've been around 30 years doing this stuff, and I've done… you know, Yardi conversions from… just from, like, whatever, to 7S.
I've done the old, old… you know, Yardi to new Yardi.
We've done, like, simple things like… … changing from Yardi S to Conservice for our utility stuff.
You know, we've rolled out new Yardi products, all kinds of stuff, and it's very difficult.
Yep.
And the one thing I do, I mean, my previous life, I was owner-manager, so it was easier, but when you're working with third-party managers, it's harder, too, when you're rolling stuff out.
Yep.

But that's… I mean, we're down to basically 3 managers, so that makes it a little more… It's easier to bite off when we do that now, and typically, Avenue 5 and us will partner and agree on something.
… So, it does make it easier than that, but it's just… it's… It's a big leap, you know?
It's a big leap.
Yeah.
It's fair.
Like, I can tell you, I would never change my operating platform from Yardi.
That would never happen.
Yeah.
That would just be… They seem to be counting on it, based on the quality of new products.
That they put out.
Yeah, I know, I'm… I'm getting the… I'm getting the demo in a couple weeks on the, … On their CRM IQ?
Hmm.
and then their AI stuff, because right now, we have… we use Yardi.
we use Yardi operating, we use NOC as our CRM.
And then we have Elise AI, which we absolutely love.
Yeah.
But the problem is, we have 3 things stacked.
So, it makes it, … The conversations aren't always that streamlined.
So, the thought is to get… maybe go to CRMIQ, At least get rid of one of those stacks.
But I don't know if it's any good.
I think Avenue 5 uses it for some of their stuff, but, you know, I'll take a look.
And then, I don't know, they were trying to tell me their AI's really good, but… I don't know.
Yeah.
The RD is so good at convincing me on something, and then once you get it, it's horrible.
Yeah.
Are they gonna try and do the same… same use case as Elise, or are they gonna try and do a slightly different use case of AI?
They say they have… they say… so we use the lease for, … for… leasing?
Right?
Not just chat box, for full leasing, full telephone, communications, maintenance orders, … Renewal, follow-up, and collections.
So we use it… we have the full package with them.
Yardi says they have the same thing.
Hmm.
So, I don't know.
Yeah, I've heard Elise is, eating everybody's lunch.
It's pretty good.
We, we love it.
And it's… it's the only… it's the first thing… It's the first real AI that I've seen that actually should help us save staffing costs.
Because….
Everybody's moving towards pods.
Nobody's… nobody… I can tell you that I've talked to a lot of different PMs.
Nobody's really got the right structure for the maintenance pod yet.

But a lot of them are doing the, like, in a system manager pod, if you will, so you take your system managers out, one person does 5 or 6 properties, but what's allowed them to be successful at that is AI, is the lease AI.
Hmm.
And, I mean, I don't know if I told you this before, Alec, but have you heard of Property Vista?
I feel like maybe?
Sure, so Property Vista is actually owned by our owner's brother.
Okay.
And it's a… it's a full CRM and AI.
And, they're trying… they're trying to, … break into the U.
S.
Alec, what's that guy's name?
Scott, you've probably heard of him.
He's like a sales….
Todd Allen lives in Vegas.
He's been around, he's, like, supposed to be, like, a sales guy legend.
he… that's who, Property Vista hired.
But he, … so we tried… we tried it for… like, 2 weeks, I… I had to call them and say, you gotta, like, I'm sorry.
you gotta get rid of this off the properties, because it wasn't… I was getting all kinds of complaints from the PMs, and….
Whatever, so….

But I used to use it in my Canadian portfolio, and it was… I mean, the CRM was… the web… it was good, but I don't think the… I think the problem is all the different plugins, you know, it's just… I don't think they've really… Got that strange note.
Anyhow….
I think I might have looked at that company.
I think when they were coming into the U.
S, I don't know if they were trying to sell?
I think they were.
He… Lenny… Lenny's the brother of Daniel.
He never really told me that, but I… I figured… the problem in Canada is they… so they do all the Canadian… we have 60,000 units in Canada.
So they do all their Canadian stuff.
Hmm.
And I was using them at maybe 10 properties in my previous life.

But everybody here never really… believe there was a Chinese wall between, you know, Starlight and Property Vista, so if you were using Property Vista, they always figured all the information was being shared with Starlight and blah blah blah.
So he had a really hard time growing it outside of the Starlight portfolio in Canada.
And I believe that's why he went to the U.
S, where nobody really knows that, right?
Interesting.
Yeah, and I… I… I got the feeling they were gonna try and build it up and then sell it.
Yeah.
Yeah, there was another brand that came in, I think they were less of a CRM.
I think they had, like, big with the ILSs in Canada?
This, this is Monopoly.
Oh, well, here it's, …
now it is a monopoly up here.
It's… I have a… I still talk to my old marketing manager from Realstar, and she says it's just terrible what they're doing, like, you have to do this, you know, you have to do this, and….
Yep.
Yeah, I can't remember the name of it.
I mean, we… I think it's Apartments.
com here, too, or something now.
Okay.
Like, somebody bought somebody, bought somebody, and then eventually you basically have one owner now.
Yeah.
Yeah, there was a decent-sized Canadian company that did ILSs, and then they did some CRM, too, but really they were an ILS business.
It was a good business, and they were trying to sell that because they wanted to… they thought they could sell it to a U.
S.
company and resell it here.
So we talked to them, but….
CoStar here, My neighbor's daughter works for CoStar.
worked for CoStar.
They laid off, like… I don't know what it was.
600 people in one day here.
Wow.
And it was all the market research department.
Oh.
And I think… I believe it's probably because, you know, nobody's sharing information, which is why we use DepartmentIQ.
That could be why.
You know, no one's answering the questions when they phone around and stuff like that.
I don't… I don't know.
I mean, I… I was telling Larissa.
I had a meeting with, … Who's your comp?
Your main comp?
I'm not saying that.
Which I said… which I always said would sell, and it did.
I'm gonna figure out.
You're right about that.
That will set you a note.
He should have, because it came up in an RNU5 meeting, and I'm like, I told you so.
Scott, we were at.
Hello, data.
Hello, data.
We're at the end.
I had a call with them.
Just to… just to have a call.
And these… you'll… you'll find this interesting.
These are the things he told me.
Why they were better.
He said they're using listing sites.
Like, they were scraping listing sites as well as the property websites?
They were picking up property fees, they had better accuracy, They were using effective rents, calculating NER, They're more accurate and easy to use.
And then they have this new underwriting thing, right?
I don't know if you know that.
They have, like, an underwriting module.
Yeah.
I'm trying to.
And push it pretty hard.
Which is not here nor… here… here nor there to me.
They said the photo quality of their properties is better.
And then they had this, you know, their new acquisition side, Pipeline Data Developments, they were getting into, you know, that, which we used to use Yardi Matrix for.
… Which, I don't even know what we use now.
I guess we use…
Real page, right, Alec?
I guess?
And then they… he promoted all this underwriting and tenure, blah blah blah.
So then I go back to your site, and I'm like, okay, well, they got NER, how can… like, I don't think anybody's occupancy is accurate, period, so how can he say that?
Like, I don't know.
Anyhow… Just give you some feedback.
It's good.
Appreciate it.
And then he followed up, and he's like, so where are we?
You know, I'm giving you a free pilot, do you want to do it?
I'm like, no, we already made a decision.
Because I had my renewal with you guys.
Makes sense.
He was trying to push a deal before that earn-out runs out, Scott, is what… what he's trying to do over there, so….
What do you mean?
Well, I think the team over there gets paid until the end of the year, based on the acquisition and the revenue.
Oh, I see.
So….
Yeah.
they didn't get bought by who I kind of thought would buy them, because they got bought… it's more of a…
… kind of an information company, is it?
Like….
Grace Hill, yeah.
Yeah.
It's like a… it's like a learning management software.
Really old business.
Yeah.
Yeah.
Yeah, that's a… that's an interesting business as well.

I don't know if you guys have ever used them, but they're, … I think 3 private equity groups have owned them in the last 6 years, and they're trying to get another turn, and… They're, they're basically a service business that's shrinking.
So everybody's freaking out, so they're trying to buy as much subscription revenue as they can possibly get their hands on.
Boost shrinking.
Grace Hill.
Great.
Really?
Their core business is shrinking, yeah.
What else do they own?
What other….
They bought HelloData, and then they recently bought another company called RealLink, which is a smaller acquisition, but that was, like, marketing.
And, we have a shared investor with Realink, so our investor knows lots about how GrayCell works.
We were pretty enthusiastic about that acquisition.
We were, … Because we were looking at it, and we were like, I don't know, it doesn't really feel like we need it.
And part of our thesis was, like, we think that they probably are gonna get acquired, the founders are gonna leave, and it's gonna die anyway.
And then Grace Hill acquired them, and we were like, that's pretty much best case.
Yeah.
So they… they… half the acquisition is the revenue they generate before the end of this year.
Well, you know… you know I even… I mean, I told… I was, like.
I was playing Avenue 5 with you guys, right?
And I'm like… and then I told them, I said, I told you it was gonna happen.
Like, I could just feel it.
Yeah, and there… so we'll see what happens, but the, … The broker that did the deal told us, the founders told him, on Jan 1, they're leaving Grace Hill.
So, we'll see what that business looks like.
But that's what happens with all this stuff, right?

You create relationships with… That's why I… I leaned more towards you guys, because I already had a relationship with you, and I knew something was going to happen, and they always say, oh, it doesn't matter.
But they always end up leaving, so… And I just think, like, just stay in your friggin' lane, that's all I want these companies to do.
Stay… do what you're good at.
Yeah.
Right?
Like, like, Yardi's into smart homes, and rev management, and this, and that, and RealPages, like….
Yep.
I don't know.
Yeah, it's … it's interesting, because multifamily is a big industry, but on the scale of, like, building a billion-dollar business.

It's actually a smaller market, and so the function it creates is you just need to launch Like, somebody like Yardia RealPage, if they're gonna get to a $10 billion, $20 billion valuation, they've gotta have 60 products.
Because the whole thing is they've got to bring that price, that dollar per unit, up.
Because there's only so many units to go sell to.
Then you get guys like… What's that valet living guy's name, Alec?
Now he's iApartments.
I mean, like, that guy created Valley Living, then he sold it, you know, I don't know, maybe he went on a yacht for 5 years, and then he created, iApartments.
And he's, you know, he's growing that, like, but at least he stayed in his lane, right?
He picked one product and grew it.
Now, I do… I did hear Valley Living sells every 4 or 5 years to a new owner, and then they kind of reset it, but… It is kind of the standard in the valet trash business, to be honest with you.
Right.
If… if you're a national… if you're a national owner.
Yep.
Sean Handerhan?
Is that him?
No, no.
He's….
West Valley, currently.
I'll tell you.
You know, Dave, … It's his last name, Dave, ….
He lives.
He's eye apartments now.
Yeah.
I think he just did an IPO.
Like, a year or so ago.
Oh, Dave Magrisso?
Think I'm pronouncing that right?
Founder of Valet Waste, now known as Valley Living, also founded Eye Apartments.
Yeah, yeah, that's him, yep.
Nice.
Yep.
Alright, well, you wanna see this thing?
Yeah, high level.
We got, 3 o'clock, we got the nice, … property tax consultants coming in from Atlanta, so… Oh, Joey Obliss.
They're taking us to the Blue Jay game, though, so that should be fun.
That's a win.
Are you just ready?
Are you going to OpTech this year?
I am, yeah.
oh my god, it's gonna be so much fun.
You know, there's Monday Night Football.
In Vegas?
Yes, Monday night, Dallas Cowboys and the Vegas, and then Tuesday night is the Golden Knights and the Rangers.
Aw, it's gonna be awesome.
That's not bad.
I don't schedule.
Alec and I aren't gonna get any work done.
I was gonna say, are we going?
Are you already going to these things, or are we going together?
We're working on it.
working on it.
Alright, well, if you want some tickets, I bet we could arrange it.
We're working on it.
Okay.
And I think we're a slightly better time than the accountants.
Yeah.
property tax consultants.
They're, you know, it kind of goes accountants, then property tax consultants.
10 salespeople, sorry, Corey, and then Hunter, and then, you know….
I was gonna say, as we transition, I'm not saying a word.
You've been pretty open about how you feel about sales guys here, so Alex?
Megan, jump in.
Let's show Scott and Alec what we got here.
I'm gonna tell you what's very impressive to me with your company, is that Alec is on… is on… The Zoom.
Well, I gotta be honest.
That is impressive, and that's what keeps me coming back.
Tellin' ya.
Well, I do give you a little bit of special treatment, too, Scott.
Well, good, because I appreciate that, and I expect it.
We appreciate the, ….
The part.
For sure.
both ways.
Alright.
Megan, let's see what you got.
High level, I get bored real easy.
Alright.
How it's gonna take… we're… we're ready.
Yeah, so… here's the crux of it.
I'll skip the slideshow, even though it maybe makes it a little bit easier, but… Here's what we heard, frustrations around existing revenue management systems.
One used to be private data, now there's no comp data in the revenue management systems.
So, we obviously, we've got all of our apartment IQ data for the last almost 5 years now.
And so, what we did is we built a new revenue management system on that public data, then we integrated the apartment IQ data directly into it, so I'll show you what that looks like.
But we trained this… the… and so then we… we took all that data and we used a new form of artificial intelligence called explainable AI, which everyone's getting used to seeing now in ChatGBT.
So you know how you can watch the models think?
It'll tell you why it made the decision it made, or what it was looking at.
that form of artificial intelligence.
You haven't seen that?
Okay.
No.
So if you use ChatGBT or Gemini, I don't… are you guys, like, a Teams office, maybe?
Microsoft office?
We're teams, yeah.

Yeah, so maybe Copilot you would see it, but all of the AI started as, it's a black box, you don't know what's going on inside of it, and now there's new forms where you can actually see what it's thinking and why.
And so, one of the.
See, I think, Alec, don't they have us locked out of GPT in the office?
No, no, I think….
There's rules and stuff, though, that… There's rules, yeah.
Because you… you can't put any… because when you… when you ask things, you could be telling it things that the whole world's gonna know, right?
Which, you know, we have public funds and the whole bit, so there was a whole thing about that.
Yep, yeah.
So… What we heard is there's no market data.
Or, previously, there was private data in RealPage, which is obviously driving a lot of folks.
The second thing we heard is…
Most revenue management is a black box, so when it makes a recommendation.
You end up having to spend a lot of time trying to figure out why it's making the recommendation it's making.
So you've either got to talk to somebody over there, or you've got to use a tool like ApartmentIQ to figure out, here's why it's recommending I drop my rents $50.
Yeah, I would say there's so many levers to pull, like with Yieldstar.
There's so many settings that I don't even know half of them.
Yeah.
Yeah.
Yeah, and that's the third point, which I know you mentioned earlier, which is the systems have become Extremely complex, the interfaces, the settings.
Makes it harder for on-site folks to get on there, so we've heard that's very complicated, and the other thing we've heard is they've gotten really expensive.
So, those were the, really, the areas that we focused on.
So, I'll take you through, I'll give you a look at the product and show you how we're trying to take on all of them.
So, this is integrated directly into the ApartmentIQ backend, so it's a new tab here, Revenue Management.

And… The first thing I should note, so ApartmentIQ, the market data is all public data, but we will integrate, we do integrate with your PMS, Yardi, sounds like you guys are a Yardi shop, to pull in your property's data, and then that data is housed completely on its own, never shared across, because if we ever did that, we'd be idiots.
So… That… so the subject property day, you're seeing the subject property here.
So, I pulled up a couple examples.
One's in Arlington, it's doing great.
The other's in Houston, it's getting some supply pressure.

So, what we try to do is combine a lot of what works about existing revenue management, so somebody like you who's been using revenue management tools, you're gonna have all the same functionality you've had.
So, expiration management, renewal flow, etc.
But we wanted to bring these new elements to it.
So, the way this thing works is similar.
You're gonna tell us a 12-week occupancy goal.
On a bed group basis, or a floor plan basis.
And so you can see that, so on the studios here, this is their goal, it's 95% on their studios, and their current occupancy is 84%.
So that would be, like, the sustain… your sustainable setting.
Exactly.
What you're trying to achieve.
Exactly.
And then you can set the time frame, but nearly everybody does 12 weeks.
The models actually do best with the 12-week time frame.
So, what the model's gonna do is it's gonna say, okay, you're at 84%, you want to be at 95%.
At your current rates.
How many leases are you going to sign?
And so it's showing that you're going to sign about 9 leases.
Over the next 12 weeks, and you need 11 to hit your goal.
So this property actually is going to make progress against their goal.
They're going to go from 84% to 92% over the next 12 weeks at their current rents, and this is a base rent, so amenity and floor plan premiums would be on top.
But they're not gonna quite get there, they're still gonna have a one-lease shortfall.

And this is where all of our… I don't know how much you get into the technical details of how these things work, Scott, but, … The core of these systems is a price elasticity curve that basically says if you change your rates by $20, you're gonna get an extra… Two leases, or you're gonna lose two leases.
And those elasticity curves now, for nearly every revenue management system, all they look at is your property's data, which we look at as well.
But that means, typically, that you really are only looking at a few data points.
maybe 5, 6, 7 data points that are actually recent and interesting on a per floor plan basis.
The difference for us is we see every data point in the market.
So we see what all your competitors are leasing at, how price changes impact that, and that's what's different about our… the actual under-the-hood model that's going on here.
So, the other thing… so, couple other quick things just to note.
